nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

nameMeanings.name: Gawthorpe
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: locative name from any of several places called Gawthorpe in Yorks and Lancs, including Gawthorpe in Osset, Gawthorpe in Lepton, a lost Gawthorpe Hall in Harewood (WR Yorks), Gowthorpe in Bishop Wilton (ER Yorks), and Gawthorpe Hall in Hagergham Eaves (Lancs), all earlier recorded as Goukthorpe. See Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames .

variants.name: Gawthrop
meanings: English (Yorkshire): habitational name from any of several places in Yorkshire and Lancashire called Gawthorpe or Gowthorpe, all of which are named from Old Norse gaukr ‘cuckoo’ + thorp ‘enclosure’.

variants.name: Gowthorpe
meanings: 1  English: locative name from Gowthorpe in Blacktoft (ER Yorks). Some of the following early bearers may alternatively belong with (2).

variants.name: Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames
meanings: English: variant of Bulcock . Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames notes that in the parish registers of Bolton by Bowland (WR Yorks) the same family name is spelled Bulcock, Bowcocke, and Boocock (1608–30), and that Edward Bowcock of Howgill (WR Yorks) is also called Bulcock in a will dated 1677. Compare also Alice Bowcock, 1641, Chr. Bulcock, 1646 in IGI (Barnoldswick, WR Yorks).

nameMeanings.name: John
meanings: Greek: the Lord gave graciously; whom Jehovah gave; whom Jehovah bestowed.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

en.lifeSummary: John of Gawthorpe was born in 1237, in Harewood, West Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 daughter. He died in Yorkshire, England.
